coredata.dc:description: While ferric (Fe3+) ions are essential nutrients for biological systems, excessive concentrations can lead to toxic effects. Therefore, it is crucial to selectively detect Fe3+ ions in industrial effluents to regulate their levels and support environmental protection efforts. This work presents the development of 2-phthalimidobenzoic acid (PBA) as a novel fluorescent sensor, which exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions. PBA was synthesized through a straightforward, environmentally friendly process without using any organic solvent or catalyst via fusion of phthalic anhydride and anthranilic acid at 135 °C for only 10 min. Structure characterization was performed using both FTIR and NMR spectroscopy, while the crystal structure and thermal stability were assessed via XRD and TGA techniques. The optical properties of PBA were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy at various pH levels. When excited at a wavelength of 336.6 nm, PBA exhibited a maximum emission peak at 405.4 nm. Using the Stern-Volmer equation, a strong correlation was observed between the concentration of Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of PBA, accompanied by a high quenching rate. The fluorescent sensor system demonstrated remarkable selectivity for Fe3+ ions over a wide range of tested metal ions and anions, with a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe3+ ions. UV–vis spectra before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions to PBA sensor exhibits a dynamic fluorescence quenching mechanism is occurring between PBA and Fe3+ ions. Additionally, DFT calculations provided atomistic insights into the sensing mechanism between Fe3+ and PBA. To further evaluate the practical applicability of the PBA sensor, five different real water samples were tested, yielding favorable results for Fe3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly one-step production of a highly sensitive fluorescent sensor for iron (III) detection in aqueous solutions: Experimental and DFT insights

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: While ferric (Fe3+) ions are essential nutrients for biological systems, excessive concentrations can lead to toxic effects. Therefore, it is crucial to selectively detect Fe3+ ions in industrial effluents to regulate their levels and support environmental protection efforts. This work presents the development of 2-phthalimidobenzoic acid (PBA) as a novel fluorescent sensor, which exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions. PBA was synthesized through a straightforward, environmentally friendly process without using any organic solvent or catalyst via fusion of phthalic anhydride and anthranilic acid at 135 °C for only 10 min. Structure characterization was performed using both FTIR and NMR spectroscopy, while the crystal structure and thermal stability were assessed via XRD and TGA techniques. The optical properties of PBA were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy at various pH levels. When excited at a wavelength of 336.6 nm, PBA exhibited a maximum emission peak at 405.4 nm. Using the Stern-Volmer equation, a strong correlation was observed between the concentration of Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of PBA, accompanied by a high quenching rate. The fluorescent sensor system demonstrated remarkable selectivity for Fe3+ ions over a wide range of tested metal ions and anions, with a significant reduction in fluorescence intensity in the presence of Fe3+ ions. UV–vis spectra before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions to PBA sensor exhibits a dynamic fluorescence quenching mechanism is occurring between PBA and Fe3+ ions. Additionally, DFT calculations provided atomistic insights into the sensing mechanism between Fe3+ and PBA. To further evaluate the practical applicability of the PBA sensor, five different real water samples were tested, yielding favorable results for Fe3+ detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is a vital nutrient in the human diet, essential for the formation of hemoglobin, which transports oxygen, and for various biological processes such as respiration and DNA replication [1–3]. Iron deficiency, prevalent in populations like adolescents and pregnant women, can lead to anemia, characterized by reduced hemoglobin and oxygen-carrying capacity [4,5]. Conversely, excess iron, particularly in the form of ferric ions, poses health risks, including organ dysfunction and increased free radicals that can damage cells [6,7]. Iron’s role extends to environmental concerns, as industrial activities and corrosion of iron pipes can contaminate water systems, threatening both human health and ecosystems [8,9]. The balance of iron levels is crucial; while deficiency can lead to serious health issues like anemia and neurodegenerative diseases, excess iron can cause oxidative stress and related diseases [10–12]. As a result, monitoring iron concentrations in biological and environmental systems is essential for preventing health hazards and maintaining ecological balance.

In recent years, various methods for detecting Fe3+ ions have been explored, including traditional techniques such as potentiometry, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic emission spectrometry (AES), atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), anodic stripping voltammetry, spectrophotometry, and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12–17]. While these methods can be sensitive and precise, they often require expensive instrumentation, trained personnel, complex sample preparations, and lengthy testing times, which limit their practical application [18,19]. In contrast, fluorescent chemical sensors have emerged as promising alternatives. These sensors are noted for their simplicity, cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid response times [20,21]. The scientific community has increasingly focused on developing fluorescence-based sensors, which facilitate easy monitoring and real-time detection, making them essential tools for reliable Fe3+ ion analysis. Such methods typically utilize turn-on/off fluorescence sensors, which rely on changes in the intensity of the fluorophore's emission upon interaction with the analyte. This interaction can either result in quenching (a decrease in fluorescence intensity) or enhancement (an increase in fluorescence intensity) of the signal.[22]. Recently, a variety of materials have been developed as fluorescent sensors for detecting Fe3+ ions. These include carbon quantum dots [5,23,24], Metal organic framework [2,25], dyes [9,26], organic polymers [27]. Recent advancements in fluorogenic chemosensors have significantly improved Fe3+ detection by enhancing selectivity and minimizing interference from other metal ions​. The development of highly sensitive fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection is therefore crucial for applications in environmental monitoring, medical diagnostics, and industrial quality control. This study presents an innovative fluorescent sensor with enhanced detection capabilities, addressing the need for efficient and selective Fe3+ monitoring in aqueous solutions [28–35].

Phthalimide derivatives have garnered significant interest across multiple applications due to their distinctive properties. They are employed as solvatofluorochromic dyes in biopolymers, enabling enhanced environmental sensitivity in molecular probes [36]. Additionally, these compounds function as oxidative agents in catalytic processes within the pharmaceutical and agrochemical sectors [37]. These derivatives exhibit highly efficient fluorescence along with relatively large Stokes shifts, making them particularly useful in various sensing applications. Fluorescent sensors based on phthalimides have been designed for the detection of cations, achiral and chiral anions, and hydrogen peroxide. Their capacity to alter fluorescence in response to environmental changes adds considerable value for real-time monitoring in chemical and biological systems. This adaptability further extends their potential in fields such as analytical chemistry and environmental monitoring, highlighting their multifunctional nature [38].

In the present study, we prepared and characterized a novel fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions based on 2-phthalimidobenzoic acid (PBA). This compound was synthesized using a straightforward, environmentally friendly one-step process that avoids organic solvents and catalysts. We evaluated its optical properties and selectivity toward Fe3+ ions in various samples. Our investigation included an exploration of the quenching mechanism through UV–vis spectroscopy, providing insights into the interaction between PBA and Fe3+ ions. Additionally, we conducted density functional theory (DFT) calculations to obtain detailed structural and electronic information about the sensing mechanism. These combined approaches contribute to a comprehensive understanding of PBA as a fluorescent sensor for Fe3+ detection, highlighting its potential for practical applications.

Phthalic anhydride and anthranilic acid, were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich ((Merck), Darmstadt, Germany). Ethanol and methanol were received from fisher scientific (Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, USA). All metal salts including AgNO3, AlCl3, BaCl2·2H2O, CaCl2, CdCl2·H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, CrCl3, CuCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4, HgCl2, KCl, Li2CO3, MgSO4, NaCl, NiCl2·6H2O, PbCl2, SnCl2·2H2O, TiCl4 and ZnCl2 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich ((Merck), Darmstadt, Germany). and fisher scientific (Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, USA). All chemicals were used as received without any further purification.

2-Phthalimidobenzoic acid PBA was synthesized by fast and green method without using any organic solvent or catalyst according to method described in Scheme1 [39]. Equimolar of phthalic anhydride (1 gm, 6.75 mmol) and anthranilic acid (0.92 gm, 6.75 mmol) was fussed at temperature equal to 135 °C for 10 min. After that, the resulting mass was directly recrystallized from ethanol and produced a white crystal with yield 94.6 % and M.p. 217–220 °C.

The detection of Fe3+ ions were performed at room temperature in a buffer solution (phosphate buffer) with a pH of 7, using PBA as the fluorophore. Sensitivity experiments to assess the response of PBA towards Fe3+ were conducted following the fluorescence titration method [20,21]. In the procedure, 500 µL of a 10−2 mol L−1 methanol solution of PBA and 1 mL of buffer solution (pH = 7) were mixed with 500 µL of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions, ranging from 0 to 100 µmol L−1. The resulting solutions were analyzed using a fluorometer with an excitation wavelength of 336.6 nm. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was determined by measuring the change in relative fluorescence intensity (I/Io) in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions. Selectivity experiments were conducted by substituting 500 µL of different metal ion solutions (10−3 mol L−1) for Fe3+ under the same experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of iron (III) ions in real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the applicability of PBA for detecting Fe3+ in real samples, we tested five different water sources: tap water, drinking water, Nile River water, Mediterranean Sea water, and treated industrial wastewater. Each water sample was spiked with Fe3+ ions. For every 1 L of water, FeCl3 (13.5–21.6 mg) was added to create five different real samples containing 50–80 µM of Fe3+ ions. The concentration of Fe3+ in these samples was determined using a standard colorimetric method with excess NH4SCN as the coloring agent [40]. These results were then compared to those obtained using PBA as a fluorescence sensor, as described in this study.

Spectroscopic data were obtained using various instruments. UV–visible spectra were recorded with a Jasco V-630 spectrophotometer, employing a 1.0 cm quartz cell over a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The fluorescence spectrum was measured using a Jasco FP-8350 spectrofluorometer, with an excitation wavelength set at 336.6 nm and an emission range from 300 to 800 nm. FTIR spectra were collected using a Jasco FTIR-4100 spectrophotometer. Samples were prepared as KBr discs, and the data were recorded in the range of 4000–400 cm−1.1H NMR and 13C NMR Analysis: Proton NMR analysis was conducted on a Bruker WP operating at 300 MHz, utilizing DMSO-d6 as the solvent and TMS as the internal reference, covering the chemical shift range of 0–10 ppm. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with a Shimadzu thermogravimetric analyzer, measuring samples from ambient temperature to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired on a Siemens D-500 X-ray diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). The scans were conducted at a rate of 10°/min across an angular range of 2θ = 3°–80°.

DFT calculation implemented in Gaussian09 [41] has been conducted to provide explanation for the sensing mechanism. In particular, B3LYP functional [42–44] in combination with 6-31 + G(d) basis set was used for all atoms except Fe(III) that has been treated by ECP LANLDZ basis set. In fact, our group has successfully applied B3LYP functional for the description of various chemical systems including Fe-containing ones [45–47]. Frequency calculations were then performed to identify the characterized complexes as stationary points with no imaginary frequency.

The structure of the synthesized PBA was confirmed using various spectroscopic techniques, including FTIR, 1HNMR and 13CNMR. Starting with the FTIR analysis, the results presented in Fig. 1 show characteristic peaks at the following wavenumbers (IR, KBr, cm−1): 3185 (OH– Carboxylic acid), 3089 (CH-aromatic), 1721(CO– Carboxylic acid), 1698 (CO– anhydride),1491 (Aromatic C=C stretching). Next, the 1HNMR spectrum (500 MHz, DMSO‑d 6), illustrated in Fig. S1 of the supplementary information, reveals specific peaks at δ 7.54(1H, dd, Ar-H), 7.62 (1H, td, Ar-H), 7.76(1H, td, Ar-H), 7.9–7.93(2H, m, Ar-H), 7.96–7.98 (2H, m, Ar-H), 8.03 (1H, dd, Ar-H), 13.13 (1H, s, COOH). Finally, the 13CNMR spectrum (500 MHz, ppm), shown in Fig. S2 of the supplementary information, displays the carbon signals of the synthesized PBA, with specific peaks at δ 167.209 for the carbonyl of imide group, 166.178 for the carbonyl of carboxylic acid,123.571, 129.265, 129.341, 130.734, 130.887, 131.096, 131.526, 131.793, 133.080 and 134.874 for the aromatic carbons.

The crystalline structure of PBA was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD), with the results illustrated in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern reveals sharp peaks at 2θ values of 11.28°, 14.15°, 15.89°, 17.91°, 18.49°, 20.92°, 21.41°, 22.50°, 24.21°, 25.58°, 26.79°, 28.53°, indicating a high degree of crystallinity in PBA. Using X’Pert HighScore Plus software, we determined the Miller indices corresponding to each diffraction peak, which were found to be (1 1 0), (0 2 0), (2 0 0), (1 1 –1), (0 0 2), (2 1 1), (2 2 0), (−3 0 2), (3 0 1), (2 0 2), (2 1 2), (3 1 1), respectively. Additionally, the crystallite size of PBA was calculated using the Scherrer equation [48–50].

where D is the crystallite size (nm), K is the Scherrer constant (0.89), λ is the wavelength of the X-ray source (0.15406 nm), β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM), and θ is the peak position. The average crystallite size was determined to be 0.971 nm. Details of the diffraction peaks, including d-values, Miller indices, net intensity, relative intensity, and crystallite size, are summarized in Table 1 .

The thermal properties of PBA (fluorophore) were investigated using thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA), with results presented in Fig. 3 .A. The TGA and differential thermal analysis (DTA) curves show a small reduction in sample weight from 60 °C to 160 °C, corresponding to a weight loss of 2 %. This initial weight loss is likely due to the evaporation of moisture or residual organic solvents remaining from the drying process. A more significant degradation stage occurs between 270 °C and 455 °C, resulting in a weight loss of 81.35 %, which is attributed to the thermal degradation of PBA. To analyze the first-order thermal degradation process, we used the Coats-Redfern equation [51–53]:

All results, including thermal activation energy, Arrhenius constant, and thermodynamic parameters for PBA, are summarized in Table 2 . Notably, both ΔG∗ and ΔH∗ exhibited positive values, indicating that the degradation process of PBA is non-spontaneous and endothermic.

The optical properties of PBA were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy in methanol as the solvent, across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 4 .A. Starting with the absorption characteristics, the results show two distinct absorption peaks at wavelengths of 232 nm and 279 nm with absorption coefficient equal to 56.13 × 103 L. Mol−1. cm−1 and 1.47 × 10 3 L. Mol−1. cm−1, respectively. The sharp peak at 232 nm is attributed to the π–π* electronic transitions of the double bonds in the aromatic rings of PBA. In contrast, the peak at 279 nm is due to n–π* electronic transitions involving the lone pair electrons on the oxygen and nitrogen functional groups in PBA. Regarding the fluorescence properties, PBA exhibits a maximum photoluminescence (PL) emission at 405.4 nm when excited at 336.6 nm, as shown in Fig. 4.A.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of PBA was determined using the comparative method, following the equation [56]:
Q s

Q r

where Qs and Qr represent the quantum yields of the sample and reference material, respectively; ms and mr denote the gradients of the plots of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance for the sample and reference; and ns and nr are the refractive indices of the solvents used for the sample and reference materials. Bovine serum albumin-stabilized copper nanoclusters (BSA-CuNCs) were selected as the reference standard due to their excitation and emission properties are similar to PBA, with a reported quantum yield of 0.1 [57] 5. Using this reference, the quantum yield of PBA was calculated to be 0.05.

Further investigations were conducted to assess the effect of pH on the fluorescence emission of PBA, using standard buffer solutions at pH values ranging from 3 to 12, with results presented in Fig. 4.B. It is evident that increasing the pH from 3 to 7 results in a marked enhancement of fluorescence intensity. However, as the pH continues to rise from 7 to 12, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. This behavior can be attributed to the deprotonation of the acid unit in PBA, which affects the electronic structure of the fluorophore. This observation aligns with findings from previous studies [20,58,59]. Therefore, the maximum fluorescence emission observed at pH 7 was identified as the optimum condition for subsequent studies.

The ability of PBA to detect Fe3+ ions was investigated using a fluorescence titration method. The influence of varying concentrations of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of PBA was assessed by recording the quenching of fluorescence intensity after the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions, as illustrated in Fig. 5 A. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of PBA is significantly quenched as the concentration of Fe3+ ions increase from 0 to 90 µM. This behavior indicates a strong interaction between PBA and Fe3+ ions, confirming its potential as a fluorescence probe for detecting Fe3+ ions. To quantify this interaction, the dependence of fluorescence quenching on the concentration of Fe3+ ions was analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation [60,61]:
F o
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ M ]
where Fo is the initial fluorescence intensity of PBA before the addition of Fe3+ ions, F is the fluorescence intensity after the addition, KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant, and [M] is the molar concentration of Fe3+ ions. Applying the Stern-Volmer equation to the experimental data resulted in a linear relationship, as shown in Fig. 5B. The excellent correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99) confirms a strong correlation between the concentration of Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of PBA. The resulting equation, (

), provides a reliable method for determining the concentration of Fe3+ ions with limit of detection (LOD) equal to 3.73 µM and limit of quantification (LOQ) equal to 11.303 µM. Additionally, the Stern-Volmer constant (KSV) was found to be 2.2 x 104 mol−1L. Further studies were conducted to assess the selectivity of PBA toward Fe3+ ions. To evaluate this, a 10−3 M solution of nineteen different cations, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Ti4+ and Zn2+, was added to PBA. The fluorescence intensity was measured before and after the addition of each metal cation, with results presented in Fig. 6 .A. The results indicate that the addition of these nineteen metal cations caused minimal quenching of the fluorescence intensity of PBA, ranging from 5 % to 24 %. In contrast, the addition of Fe3+ ions led to a significant quenching of up to 78 % under the same conditions. The superior selectivity of our sensor for Fe(III) over non-transition metal ions can be attributed to the presence of empty d-orbitals, which facilitate ligand-to-metal charge transfer [62]. Additionally, the higher oxidation state of Fe(III) compared to other transition metals such as Cu(II), Ni(II), Pb(II), Co(II), Zn(II), Sn(II), Hg(II), Cd(II), and Fe(II) enhances its electrostatic interactions, making it more favorable for detection [63]. Furthermore, the stronger affinity of the sensor toward Fe(III) over Cr(III) and Ti(IV) likely arises from the specific nature of the oxygen coordinating in the ligand as a hard base, which exhibit a preference for hard acid Fe ions [64]. Additionally, the selectivity studies were expanded via evaluation of the interfering effect of various anions including F−, SO4 2−, HCO2 –, S2 −, CrO4 2−, NO3 –, NO2 –, CO3 2–, CN–, H2PO4 −, and SCN− (Fig. 6.B). The results indicate that the presence of these anions does not cause any significant interference. These findings confirm the high selectivity of PBA for ferric (III) ions, highlighting its potential applicability as a selective sensor for Fe3+ ions.

Fluorescence quenching can be categorized into two primary mechanisms. Dynamic quenching occurs when the excited fluorophore collides with a quencher during its excited state lifetime, resulting in non-radiative deactivation and a subsequent reduction in fluorescence intensity. In contrast, static quenching involves the formation of a non-radiative complex between the fluorophore and the quencher in the ground state, inhibiting fluorescence emission before excitation. To determine the mechanism of quenching in PBA upon the addition of Fe3+ ions, UV–vis spectra were recorded before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions, with results presented in Fig. 7 . The spectra show that the absorption peaks of PBA at wavelengths of 232 nm and 279 nm do not exhibit blue or red shifts, and their intensities increase with the addition of Fe3+ ions. This behavior confirms that dynamic fluorescence quenching is occurring between PBA and Fe3+ ions. According to this mechanism, the excited PBA (the fluorophore) collides with Fe3+ ions (the quencher) during its excited state lifetime, leading to energy or electron transfer that results in the non-radiative deactivation of PBA. Similar behaviors have been reported in other studies [65,66].

Initially, the fluorescence sensor has been optimized and different possible conformation have been considered. The most stable conformation with the lowest energy amongst a total of four plausible conformations is found to be conformer a, Fig. 8 .a. Similarly, the complex containing Fe(III) ions in aqueous medium was assumed to be hexacoordinated with water molecule, [Fe(H2O)6]3+. Due to the open shell characteristic of ferric ions, it can possibly exist in three different spin states namely, doublet, quartet and sextet. After considering the three possible spin stats, the sextet multiplicity has been identified as the most stable one. Later, the most favorable conformation for the sensor together with the aqueous complex of Fe(III) in the most probable multiplicity has been used to propose a possible sensing mechanism, Fig. 9 .

There are various possible scenarios for the fluorescence quenching including Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), Photoinduced Electron Transfer, Excited state intramolecular proton transfer (EIPT) or Ligand-to-metal charge transfer (LMCT). ESIPT is a common quenching pathway given the chemical structure of our sensor; however, the possibility of this mechanism to take place could be ruled out due to its absence with all the invistigated metal ions except Fe(III). To better elucidate the mechanism, the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) for both molecules have been displayed in Fig. 9. In the same figure, a schematic representation for their energy levels values is also depicted to enhance our understanding of the possible electronic configurations for the excited electrons. According to the HOMO of the PBA sensor, it is observed that the conjugation system of the aromatic groups contributes mainly for the orbital distribution. On the other hand, the LUMO is noticed to predominantly present over the right-side aromatic moiety of the sensor. For the [Fe(H2O)6]3+, the HOMO is found to be delocalized on the coordinating oxygen atoms originated from the water molecules whereas its LUMO is distributed over the entire metal complex. The energy levels for the HOMO and LUMO have been represented schematically in Fig. 9 Upon photon absorption, an electronic transition will take place at the PBA sensor where an electron will be transited from the HOMO into the LUMO. Simultaneously, this excited electron should be relaxed back into the ground state and thus a fluorescence should take place by the emitted photons. However, due to the presence of [Fe(H2O)6]3+ with its lower LUMO the relaxation mechanism experiences a dramatic change that should affect its regular fluorescence behavior. Specifically, the excited electron could possibly be relaxed to the LUMO of [Fe(H2O)6]3+, instead of the corresponding one for the sensor due to its much less energy value, Fig. 9. In another words, the fluorescence quenching mechanism could be explained by a ligand-to-metal charge transfer. Specifically, a charge transfer from the sensor to the unfilled d orbital of Fe3+ that reduces the luminescence intensity. This alternative pathway for the electron relaxation could be the reason for the obtained quenching of the default florescence. Overall, the sensing mechanism of Fe(III) is explained by the contribution of its empty LUMO during the electron relaxation. This proposed scenario is supported by the significant delocalization of the LUMO over the entire [Fe(H2O)6]3+, complex.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection analysis of ferric ion in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the PBA fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions was evaluated using real water samples containing Fe3+. Initially, the concentration of Fe3+ ions in these samples was determined using a standard colorimetric method with NH4SCN as the coloring agent (see Fig. S3 in the supplementary information for the calibration curve) [40]. Subsequently, the PBA sensor was employed to determine the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the same water samples. Table 3 compares the concentrations of Fe3+ ions obtained from the standard colorimetric method and the PBA sensor. The results indicate that the PBA probe successfully recovered 98.41 %, 104.79 %, 121.57 %, 102.79 %, and 109.9 % of Fe3+ ions from tap water, drinking water, river water, seawater, and treated water samples, respectively. These findings confirm that PBA can serve as an efficient fluorescent sensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions in real water samples.

To assess the reliability of the PBA sensor for Fe3+ ion detection, a statistical comparison was conducted against the standard colorimetric method. The f-test was first applied to compare the variances of the two methods. The obtained F-value of 0.311 and a corresponding p-value of 0.142 indicate that there is no significant difference in the variability of measurements between the two techniques. This suggests that the precision of the PBA sensor is comparable to that of the standard colorimetric method. Additionally, a t-test was performed to evaluate whether there was a significant difference between the mean Fe3+ ion concentrations measured by both methods. The t-test resulted in a t-statistic of −1.623 and a p-value of 0.153, confirming that the differences between the two methods are not statistically significant (p > 0.05). This indicates that the PBA sensor provides Fe3+ concentration measurements that are statistically equivalent to those obtained using the standard method. Overall, these statistical analyses confirm the accuracy and reproducibility of the PBA sensor for Fe3+ detection in real water samples. The strong agreement between the two methods suggests that the PBA sensor can serve as a reliable alternative for Fe3+ ion quantification, offering potential advantages in terms of simplicity and sensitivity.

Table 4 illustrates a comparison of our fluorescent sensor for detecting Fe3+ ions with other sensors reported in the literature. Notably, the PBA fluorescent sensor stands out as one of the most effective sensors for detecting ferric ions, with a LOD of 3.73 µM and a linear range extending from 0 up to 90 µM. These results align with government standards, which set the allowable concentration of Fe3+ in drinking water at 0.3 mg/L [5]. This highlights the sensor's effectiveness and suitability for monitoring Fe3+ levels in environmental and health-related applications.

In this study, we successfully identified 2-phthalimidobenzoic acid (PBA) as a novel fluorescent sensor for detecting Fe3+ ions. PBA was synthesized through a straightforward, environmentally friendly one-step process that eliminates the need for organic solvents and catalysts. XRD analysis revealed that PBA possesses a highly crystalline structure, while TGA demonstrated thermal stability up to 560 K. Our findings indicate that Fe3+ ions can be effectively detected at low concentrations, ranging from 0 to 90 µM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.73 µM. The excellent correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99) derived from the Stern-Volmer equation confirms a strong relationship between the concentration of Fe3+ ions and the fluorescence intensity of PBA, providing a reliable and straightforward method for quantifying Fe3+ levels. Additionally, UV–vis spectroscopy studies before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions suggest a dynamic fluorescence quenching mechanism occurring between PBA and Fe3+ ions. Notably, the PBA probe successfully recovered 98.41 %, 104.79 %, 121.57 %, 102.79 %, and 109.9 % of Fe3+ ions from tap water, drinking water, river water, seawater, and treated water samples, respectively. These results confirm that PBA serves as an efficient fluorescent sensor for detecting Fe3+ in real water samples, contributing significantly to efforts aimed at ensuring a clean environment. DFT calculations were then performed to better underline the sensing mechanism and propose a strategy for Fe(III) detection. The ELUMO for the Fe3+ in complex with water molecules is found to be a better accommodation energy level for the excited electron from the fluorophore. This alternative pathway for the relaxation of the electron is suggested to play a role in the sensing mechanism. Overall, our combined computational and experimental studies identified a remarkably sensitive, green, easy to prepare sensor for detecting Fe3+ in real water samples.
